---
title: "nitrix.stats.glmm"
generated: true
signature-hash: 8e34668d301dfb66
description: "Mass-univariate generalised **linear mixed** models (GLMM) via PQL."
listing:
  id: nitrix-stats-glmm-symbols
  contents: "nitrix-stats-glmm/*.qmd"
  type: table
  fields: [title, description]
  sort: "title"
  filter-ui: false
  page-size: 500
---

::: {.callout-important title="Generated reference stub — fill me"}
Auto-generated scaffold for `nitrix.stats.glmm`. Fill the prose *outside* the
generated markers; do **not** ship as-is (SPEC §6.5). `check_reference_staleness.py` flags signature drift.
:::

<!-- nitrix:gen overview:__module__ -->
Mass-univariate generalised **linear mixed** models (GLMM) via PQL.

[`glmm_fit`](nitrix-stats-glmm/glmm_fit.qmd) fits, per element (voxel / vertex / fixel), a random-intercept
GLMM under a non-Gaussian [`Family`](nitrix-stats-glm/Family.qmd):

`$text
g(E[y | b]) = X \beta + b[group],   b_j ~ N(0, \sigma_b^2),   j = 1 .. q$`

by **penalised quasi-likelihood** (PQL; Breslow & Clayton, 1993): an outer IRLS
loop forms the working response $z$ and weights $W$ from the current
fit, then a single variance-component (Fellner-Schall) step updates the
random-effect precision $\lambda = \phi / \sigma_b^2$ -- the "penalised GLM
= variance-components REML" identity, here under a GLM family.  This is the
estimator `mgcv::gam(family=, s(g, bs="re"))` / `glmmPQL` (with the documented
PQL attenuation for binary / low-count responses; a Laplace approximation is the
more accurate follow-up).
<!-- nitrix:/gen -->

<!-- nitrix:gen sections:__module__ -->
### Performance preserving dispatch

A random effect widens the per-voxel system by $q$ = #levels, so the dense
penalised solve is $O((p + q)^3)$ per voxel.  That is **cheap for few-level
factors** (site / scanner / batch, $q \sim 10\text{--}50$) but a latency
cliff for **many-level** factors (random intercept per *subject*,
$q \sim 100\text{--}1000$).  [`glmm_fit`](nitrix-stats-glmm/glmm_fit.qmd) therefore dispatches on the
level count:

- **few-level** (`q <= few_level_max`): the dense GAMM path --
  [`gam_fit`](nitrix-stats-gam/gam_fit.qmd) with a
  [`re_smooth`](nitrix-stats-basis/re_smooth.qmd) block and the GLM family.  This *is* the
  optimal solver in this regime (no cheaper exact route), and reuses the shipped
  GAM machinery verbatim.
- **many-level** (`q > few_level_max`): a **structured** PQL that never forms
  the $(p + q) \times (p + q)$ system.  Because a single grouping factor
  makes the random-effect block of the normal equations **diagonal across
  groups**, the Schur complement onto the $p$-dimensional fixed block costs
  $O(N p^2 + q)$ per voxel (the block-Woodbury structure, weighted by the
  IRLS $W$ and wrapped in the PQL loop) -- linear in the level count, and
  free of cuSOLVER.

The two paths run the *same* PQL iteration (same working response, same
Fellner-Schall update, same iteration budget), so they agree to the iterative
tolerance; the dispatch only changes the linear-algebra cost, never the answer.

Scope.  Beyond the **scalar random intercept** `(1 | g)` (binary outcomes /
lesion counts per subject with random intercepts), [`glmm_fit`](nitrix-stats-glmm/glmm_fit.qmd) also fits
non-Gaussian random **slopes** via the `z` / `structure` arguments: diagonal
`(x || g)` as independent [`re_smooth`](nitrix-stats-basis/re_smooth.qmd) blocks through
[`gam_fit`](nitrix-stats-gam/gam_fit.qmd), and correlated `(1 + x | g)` via the
block-Woodbury REML wrapped in the PQL loop (the penalised-IRLS =
iteratively-reweighted-REML identity).  The Gaussian-family slope fit is the same
REML estimator as [`lme_fit`](nitrix-stats-lme.qmd) (block-Woodbury), to the
iterative (optimiser) tolerance.  Random slopes are also served under the
**Laplace** marginal likelihood (`method='laplace'`, the $r$-dimensional
conditional-mode integral + $r \times r$ determinant correction), which
corrects the PQL attenuation for binary / low-count slopes.

### References

- Breslow, N. E. & Clayton, D. G. (1993). Approximate inference in generalised
  linear mixed models.  Journal of the American Statistical Association, 88,
  9-25.  `10.2307/2290687`
- Wood, S. N. & Fasiolo, M. (2017). A generalised Fellner-Schall method for
  smoothing parameter optimisation with application to Tweedie location, scale
  and shape models.  Biometrics, 73, 1071-1081.  `10.1111/biom.12666`
<!-- nitrix:/gen -->

<!-- nitrix:gen dims:__page__ -->
**Tensor dimensions**

| dim | meaning |
|---|---|
| `V` | elements of a mass-univariate map — voxels, vertices, or fixels |
| `p` | fixed-effect coefficients / design-matrix columns |
| `q` | random-effect columns |
| `r` | *(undocumented — add to `_dimensions.yml`)* |
| `N` | observations — time points, subjects, or sampled points |
<!-- nitrix:/gen -->

## Symbols

::: {#nitrix-stats-glmm-symbols}
:::
